[2026-07-04 22:48:00 INFO] Team leader: **[Start Chat]**

[You're a process design reviewer. Now, we share a common interest in collaborating to successfully complete a task assigned by a new customer.
You are familiar with exclusive gateway, parallel gateway and inclusive gateway in BPMN. Your main responsibilities include give suggestion to the process.
Here is a new customer's task: Module 2: Early Treatment
This module identifies best practices for the ED treatment of TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke. The
majority of TIA patients and some patients with minor stroke do not require admission to hospital
and should be referred to an urgent TIA/minor stroke unit/TIA clinic/stroke-prevention clinic or
comparable ambulatory care setting for rapid diagnostic and medical evaluation. All patients should
be given appropriate cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies (Modules 5 and 10).
Module 2 Recommended Practices 
2.1 Glucose management
2.1.1 Hypoglycemia should be corrected immediately
2.1.2 Blood glucose measurement should be repeated if the first
random glucose value is > 11 mmol/L
2.2 Body temperature
2.2.1 Temperature should be evaluated as part of routine vital signs
every 4 hours for first 48 hours
For temperature > 37.5 C:
• initiate temperature-reducing measures
• investigate potential infection
• initiate antipyretic and antimicrobial therapy as required
2.3 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies
2.3.1 All patients, whether admitted to hospital or discharged, should
be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary prevention
assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10)
2.4 Management of patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
2.4.1 The following OHTAC recommendations should be followed:
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA with high-
risk featuresa or a minor strokeb undergo a brain CT scan and
initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated)
as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset,
followed by other stroke-prevention treatments tailored to each
patient. With respect to the location of care, OHTAC recommends
that:
o such immediate care be provided at a specialized TIA/minor
stroke clinicc
o where delays to accessing a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic
pose risks to patient health, evaluation (as outlined above) occur
at an appropriately resourced ED, and further consideration be
given to inpatient evaluation and management for stroke
prevention. OHTAC further recommends the establishment of
accreditation standards for TIA/minor stroke care to ensure
equitable access to appropriate, high-quality care, irrespective of
the location of initial presentation
o where medical attention has been sought after 48 hours from
symptom onset, patients be referred for evaluation at a
specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic or alternatively an outpatient
clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd within 24 hours of initial
presentation
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA without
high-risk featuresa undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of
antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as
possible and no later than 24 hours after initial presentation,
followed by referral to an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention
servicesd for comprehensive evaluation and management within 1
month of symptom onset
2.4.2 Some TIA patients, such as those who present with fluctuating or
crescendo hemibodye motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech
difficulty, should be evaluated in the ED to be considered for admission
to hospital
2.4.3 Patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke who are not on
an antiplatelet agent at the time of presentation should be started on
antiplatelet therapy immediately with 1 of the following after brain
imaging has excluded intracranial hemorrhage:
• ASA 160 mg loading dose, followed by enteric coated ASA (81–
325 mg) daily. Most patients should be on a maintenance dose of
81 mg/day
• clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose, followed by 75 mg/day
• extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg/ASA 25 mg bid (load with
ASA 160 mg first)
2.4.4 Rectal or gastric ASA should be offered as necessary, such as
for patients who fail swallowing screening
2.5–2.8 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; Australia, Australian Clinical analysis; ED, emergency department; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; OHTAC, Ontario Health Technology Advisory Committee; NHS/NICE,
National Collaborating Centre for Chronic Conditions; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack.
aSymptoms consistent with a hemispheric event, including sudden hemiparesis, speech difficulties, or monocular vision loss, and/or known high
risk conditions associated with stroke, including atrial fibrillation (especially if inadequately anticoagulated) or known carotid artery
atherosclerosis with > 50% stenosis (narrowing) on the side consistent with the hemispheric event.
bPatients with very mild persistent symptoms or no residual symptoms but with a small asymptomatic infarct (stroke) on imaging
cA clinic with stroke expertise and the resources to conduct all necessary investigations in 1 place to initiate rapid treatment, including brain and
vascular imaging, heart monitoring, and laboratory tests.
dFor example, a provincial stroke-prevention clinic or community neurology/internal medicine clinic with a stroke-prevention focus.
eMotor weakness may be in 1 body segment (face, arm, or leg), but sensory loss must involve at least 2 contiguous segments (face/arm or
arm/leg) on 1 side of the body to be considered highest risk.

The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel.
Module 2 Implementation Considerations
General considerations
• Standardized priority protocols should be established for all suspected stroke patients to receive treatment as soon as
possible and be admitted to a stroke unit within the first few hours after presenting to an ED
• Hospitals should have ready access to rectal ASA
Abbreviations: ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; ED, emergency department..
To complete the task, I will give you one or more instructions, and you must help me to write a specific solution that appropriately solves the requested instruction based on your expertise and my needs.]

task: Module 2: Early Treatment
This module identifies best practices for the ED treatment of TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke. The
majority of TIA patients and some patients with minor stroke do not require admission to hospital
and should be referred to an urgent TIA/minor stroke unit/TIA clinic/stroke-prevention clinic or
comparable ambulatory care setting for rapid diagnostic and medical evaluation. All patients should
be given appropriate cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies (Modules 5 and 10).
Module 2 Recommended Practices 
2.1 Glucose management
2.1.1 Hypoglycemia should be corrected immediately
2.1.2 Blood glucose measurement should be repeated if the first
random glucose value is > 11 mmol/L
2.2 Body temperature
2.2.1 Temperature should be evaluated as part of routine vital signs
every 4 hours for first 48 hours
For temperature > 37.5 C:
• initiate temperature-reducing measures
• investigate potential infection
• initiate antipyretic and antimicrobial therapy as required
2.3 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies
2.3.1 All patients, whether admitted to hospital or discharged, should
be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary prevention
assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10)
2.4 Management of patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
2.4.1 The following OHTAC recommendations should be followed:
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA with high-
risk featuresa or a minor strokeb undergo a brain CT scan and
initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated)
as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset,
followed by other stroke-prevention treatments tailored to each
patient. With respect to the location of care, OHTAC recommends
that:
o such immediate care be provided at a specialized TIA/minor
stroke clinicc
o where delays to accessing a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic
pose risks to patient health, evaluation (as outlined above) occur
at an appropriately resourced ED, and further consideration be
given to inpatient evaluation and management for stroke
prevention. OHTAC further recommends the establishment of
accreditation standards for TIA/minor stroke care to ensure
equitable access to appropriate, high-quality care, irrespective of
the location of initial presentation
o where medical attention has been sought after 48 hours from
symptom onset, patients be referred for evaluation at a
specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic or alternatively an outpatient
clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd within 24 hours of initial
presentation
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA without
high-risk featuresa undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of
antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as
possible and no later than 24 hours after initial presentation,
followed by referral to an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention
servicesd for comprehensive evaluation and management within 1
month of symptom onset
2.4.2 Some TIA patients, such as those who present with fluctuating or
crescendo hemibodye motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech
difficulty, should be evaluated in the ED to be considered for admission
to hospital
2.4.3 Patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke who are not on
an antiplatelet agent at the time of presentation should be started on
antiplatelet therapy immediately with 1 of the following after brain
imaging has excluded intracranial hemorrhage:
• ASA 160 mg loading dose, followed by enteric coated ASA (81–
325 mg) daily. Most patients should be on a maintenance dose of
81 mg/day
• clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose, followed by 75 mg/day
• extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg/ASA 25 mg bid (load with
ASA 160 mg first)
2.4.4 Rectal or gastric ASA should be offered as necessary, such as
for patients who fail swallowing screening
2.5–2.8 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; Australia, Australian Clinical analysis; ED, emergency department; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; OHTAC, Ontario Health Technology Advisory Committee; NHS/NICE,
National Collaborating Centre for Chronic Conditions; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack.
aSymptoms consistent with a hemispheric event, including sudden hemiparesis, speech difficulties, or monocular vision loss, and/or known high
risk conditions associated with stroke, including atrial fibrillation (especially if inadequately anticoagulated) or known carotid artery
atherosclerosis with > 50% stenosis (narrowing) on the side consistent with the hemispheric event.
bPatients with very mild persistent symptoms or no residual symptoms but with a small asymptomatic infarct (stroke) on imaging
cA clinic with stroke expertise and the resources to conduct all necessary investigations in 1 place to initiate rapid treatment, including brain and
vascular imaging, heart monitoring, and laboratory tests.
dFor example, a provincial stroke-prevention clinic or community neurology/internal medicine clinic with a stroke-prevention focus.
eMotor weakness may be in 1 body segment (face, arm, or leg), but sensory loss must involve at least 2 contiguous segments (face/arm or
arm/leg) on 1 side of the body to be considered highest risk.

The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel.
Module 2 Implementation Considerations
General considerations
• Standardized priority protocols should be established for all suspected stroke patients to receive treatment as soon as
possible and be admitted to a stroke unit within the first few hours after presenting to an ED
• Hospitals should have ready access to rectal ASA
Abbreviations: ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; ED, emergency department.
process text:
<process>
  <activity role='ED team' action='assess suspected TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke and determine symptom onset time and initial clinical status' objects='patient history and examination findings' id='activity1'/>
  <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway1'>
    <branch id='branch1'>
      <activity role='nursing staff' action='measure random blood glucose at presentation' objects='blood glucose sample' id='activity2'/>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway1'>
        <branch condition='hypoglycemia is present' id='branch4'>
          <activity role='ED clinician' action='correct hypoglycemia immediately' objects='hypoglycemia treatment' id='activity3'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='first random glucose value is greater than 11 mmol/L' id='branch5'>
          <activity role='nursing staff' action='repeat blood glucose measurement' objects='repeat blood glucose sample' id='activity4'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='first random glucose value is 11 mmol/L or less and hypoglycemia is not present' id='branch6'>
          <activity role='nursing staff' action='document glucose result and continue routine monitoring' objects='blood glucose result' id='activity5'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch2'>
      <activity role='nursing staff' action='evaluate temperature at presentation as part of routine vital signs' objects='temperature record' id='activity6'/>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway2'>
        <branch condition='temperature is greater than 37.5 C' id='branch7'>
          <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway2'>
            <branch id='branch9'>
              <activity role='nursing staff' action='initiate temperature-reducing measures' objects='temperature-reducing measures' id='activity7'/>
            </branch>
            <branch id='branch10'>
              <activity role='ED clinician' action='investigate potential infection' objects='infection workup' id='activity8'/>
            </branch>
          </parallelGateway>
          <inclusiveGateway id='inclusivegateway1'>
            <branch condition='antipyretic therapy is required' id='branch11'>
              <activity role='ED clinician' action='initiate antipyretic therapy' objects='antipyretic medication' id='activity9'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='antimicrobial therapy is required' id='branch12'>
              <activity role='ED clinician' action='initiate antimicrobial therapy' objects='antimicrobial medication' id='activity10'/>
            </branch>
          </inclusiveGateway>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='temperature is 37.5 C or less' id='branch8'>
          <activity role='nursing staff' action='document temperature result and continue clinical observation' objects='temperature record' id='activity11'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch3'>
      <activity role='stroke clinician' action='assess for high-risk TIA features and minor stroke findings' objects='neurologic findings and risk features' id='activity12_1'/>
      <activity role='stroke clinician' action='assess for fluctuating or crescendo neurologic symptoms' objects='symptom pattern and neurologic changes' id='activity12_2'/>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway3'>
        <branch condition='patient has TIA with high-risk features or minor (nondisabling) stroke' id='branch13'>
          <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway15'>
            <branch condition='TIA has fluctuating or crescendo hemibody motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech difficulty' id='branch50'>
              <activity role='ED clinician' action='assess symptom fluctuation or crescendo pattern in the ED' objects='serial neurologic assessment findings' id='activity13_1'/>
              <activity role='ED clinician' action='determine whether hospital admission should be considered' objects='admission assessment' id='activity13_2'/>
              <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway16'>
                <branch condition='hospital admission or immediate inpatient evaluation is needed after ED assessment' id='branch52'>
                  <activity role='ED clinician' action='initiate inpatient evaluation and management for stroke prevention' objects='inpatient stroke-prevention plan' id='activity45'/>
                  <activity role='stroke team' action='request urgent brain CT scan' objects='brain CT order' id='activity46'/>
                  <activity role='radiology team' action='perform brain CT scan within the required timeframe' objects='brain CT scan' id='activity47'/>
                  <activity role='stroke team' action='review brain imaging for intracranial hemorrhage exclusion' objects='brain imaging results' id='activity48'/>
                  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway17'>
                    <branch condition='intracranial hemorrhage is excluded, patient is not on an antiplatelet agent at presentation, and antiplatelet therapy is not contraindicated' id='branch54'>
                      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway18'>
                        <branch condition='patient fails swallowing screening or cannot take oral medication' id='branch55'>
                          <activity role='ED clinician' action='offer rectal or gastric ASA immediately' objects='rectal ASA or gastric ASA' id='activity49'/>
                        </branch>
                        <branch condition='patient passes swallowing screening' id='branch56'>
                          <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway19'>
                            <branch condition='ASA regimen is selected' id='branch57'>
                              <activity role='ED clinician' action='administer ASA 160 mg loading dose immediately followed by enteric coated ASA 81-325 mg daily, with most patients maintained on 81 mg/day' objects='ASA regimen' id='activity50'/>
                            </branch>
                            <branch condition='clopidogrel regimen is selected' id='branch58'>
                              <activity role='ED clinician' action='administer clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose immediately followed by 75 mg/day' objects='clopidogrel regimen' id='activity51'/>
                            </branch>
                            <branch condition='extended-release dipyridamole and ASA regimen is selected' id='branch59'>
                              <activity role='ED clinician' action='administer ASA 160 mg loading dose immediately then extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg and ASA 25 mg twice daily' objects='extended-release dipyridamole and ASA regimen' id='activity52'/>
                            </branch>
                          </exclusiveGateway>
                        </branch>
                      </exclusiveGateway>
                    </branch>
                    <branch condition='intracranial hemorrhage is not excluded, or patient is already on an antiplatelet agent at presentation, or antiplatelet therapy is contraindicated' id='branch60'>
                      <activity role='stroke clinician' action='review current antiplatelet therapy or withhold antiplatelet as indicated and tailor stroke-prevention treatment' objects='medication history, contraindications, and brain imaging results' id='activity53'/>
                    </branch>
                  </exclusiveGateway>
                  <activity role='stroke clinician' action='initiate other stroke-prevention treatments tailored to each patient' objects='individualized stroke-prevention treatment plan' id='activity54'/>
                  <activity role='ED clinician' action='admit patient or continue inpatient evaluation and management for stroke prevention' objects='hospital admission and inpatient stroke-prevention plan' id='activity55'/>
                  <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway5'>
                    <branch id='branch61'>
                      <activity role='nursing staff' action='evaluate temperature every 4 hours for the first 48 hours during admission' objects='inpatient temperature record' id='activity56'/>
                      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway24'>
                        <branch condition='temperature during inpatient monitoring is greater than 37.5 C' id='branch62'>
                          <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway6'>
                            <branch id='branch65'>
                              <activity role='nursing staff' action='initiate temperature-reducing measures during admission' objects='temperature-reducing measures' id='activity57'/>
                            </branch>

For the modification scheme, the specific reason for modification and the details of the modification scheme should be explained.
Here's a shipping process and some examples of checking for errors, you can refer but not copy:
<process>
  <activity role='Sender' action='fill in the shipping information' objects='package, address, contact information' id='activity1'/>
  <activity role='Courier' action='receiving package' objects='package' id='activity2'/>
  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway1'>
    <branch condition='select pick-up' id='branch1'>
      <activity role='Sender' action='pick-up arrangement' objects='package' id='activity3'/>
    </branch>
    <branch condition='choose to send by yourself' id='branch2'>
      <activity role='Sender' action='go to the mailing point to send' objects='package,mailing point address' id='activity4'/>
    </branch>
  </exclusiveGateway>
  <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway1'>
    <branch id='branch3'>
      <activity role='Express company' action='packing and handling packages' objects='package' id='activity5'/>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch4'>
      <activity role='Express company' action='transport and distribution package' objects='package' id='activity6'/>
    </branch>
  </parallelGateway>
  <sequenceGateway id='sequencegateway1'>
    <activity role='Express company' action='end of service' objects='' id='activity7'/>
    <activity role='recipient' action='sign for package' objects='package' id='activity8'/>
  </sequenceGateway>
</process>
example1:
   Problem: The 'receiving package' activity with id='activity2' and the exclusive gateway with id='exclusivegateway1' are in the wrong order and logic error.
   Suggested modification: Move the 'receiving package' activity with id='activity2' under the branch with id='branch1' of the exclusive gateway with id='exclusivegateway1'.
   Reason: After the sender chooses to pick up the package, the Courier can perform the activities of accepting the package.
example2:
   Problem: The activities are not a parallel relation in the parallel gateway with id='parallelgateway1'.
   Suggested modification: In the parallel gateway with id='parallelgateway1', change the activities from a parallel structure to a sequential structure.
   Reason: Logically, 'packing and handling packages' and 'transport and distribution packages' are not parallel relationships.
example3:
   Problem: The 'end of service' activity with id='activity7' and the 'sign for parcel' activity with id='activity8' are in the wrong order.
   Suggested modification: Swap the position of the 'end of service' activity with id='activity7' and the 'sign for parcel' activity with id='activity8'.
   Reason: Only after the recipient sign for package can the express company terminate the service.
example4:
   Problem: The sequence gateway with id='sequencegateway1' is incorrectly formatted.
   Suggested modification: Remove the gateway label of the sequence gateway with id='sequencegateway1'.
   Reason: Sequential activities need not be contained under a gateway.
When making recommendations, don't forget the format of the process text:

When the process starts, please output '<process>'
An activity is represented as follows:
<activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
The exclusive gateway is represented as follows:
<exclusiveGateway id=''>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</exclusiveGateway>
The parallel gateway is represented as follows:
<parallelGateway id=''>
  <branch id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</parallelGateway>
The inclusive gateway is represented as follows:
<inclusiveGateway id=''>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
  <branch condition='' id=''>
    <activity role='' action='' objects='' id=''/>
  </branch>
</inclusiveGateway>
At the end of the process, please output '</process>'

There are a few requirements that should be noted when generating process text:
    1) role, action, objects and id in an activity represent the subject of the activity, the activity behavior, the items needed to perform in the activity and the activity id, respectively.
    2) There are at least two branches within the exclusive gateway and the parallel gateway.
    3) Activity, gateway, and branch id attributes must be unique.
    4) When the branch in the exclusive gateway has a condition, fill the condition into the condition attribute. When the branch unconditional within the exclusive gateway, the condition attribute of the branch =''. However, all branches within a exclusive gateway can only be conditional, or both unconditional.
    5) The branch in the parallel gateway has no condition attribute.
    6) Each branch in the inclusive gateway must have condition.
    7) There must be at least one activity inside the branch tag.
    8) The activity of a sequential relation need not be represented by a gateway, that is, there is no 'sequenceGateway' label.

Please note:
1) If you think there is an error in the process text, output the error as requested. Please Just list one for me. 
2) If you think there is no formatting or logical error in the process text, please just output 'There is no error in the process text.' and don't say anything else.

[2026-07-04 22:48:01 INFO] Process design expert: **Process design expert<->Process design reviewer on : ProcessReview, turn 0**

[You're a process design reviewer. Now, we share a common interest in collaborating to successfully complete a task assigned by a new customer.
You are familiar with exclusive gateway, parallel gateway and inclusive gateway in BPMN. Your main responsibilities include give suggestion to the process.
Here is a new customer's task: Module 2: Early Treatment
This module identifies best practices for the ED treatment of TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke. The
majority of TIA patients and some patients with minor stroke do not require admission to hospital
and should be referred to an urgent TIA/minor stroke unit/TIA clinic/stroke-prevention clinic or
comparable ambulatory care setting for rapid diagnostic and medical evaluation. All patients should
be given appropriate cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies (Modules 5 and 10).
Module 2 Recommended Practices 
2.1 Glucose management
2.1.1 Hypoglycemia should be corrected immediately
2.1.2 Blood glucose measurement should be repeated if the first
random glucose value is > 11 mmol/L
2.2 Body temperature
2.2.1 Temperature should be evaluated as part of routine vital signs
every 4 hours for first 48 hours
For temperature > 37.5 C:
• initiate temperature-reducing measures
• investigate potential infection
• initiate antipyretic and antimicrobial therapy as required
2.3 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies
2.3.1 All patients, whether admitted to hospital or discharged, should
be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary prevention
assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10)
2.4 Management of patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
2.4.1 The following OHTAC recommendations should be followed:
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA with high-
risk featuresa or a minor strokeb undergo a brain CT scan and
initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated)
as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset,
followed by other stroke-prevention treatments tailored to each
patient. With respect to the location of care, OHTAC recommends
that:
o such immediate care be provided at a specialized TIA/minor
stroke clinicc
o where delays to accessing a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic
pose risks to patient health, evaluation (as outlined above) occur
at an appropriately resourced ED, and further consideration be
given to inpatient evaluation and management for stroke
prevention. OHTAC further recommends the establishment of
accreditation standards for TIA/minor stroke care to ensure
equitable access to appropriate, high-quality care, irrespective of
the location of initial presentation
o where medical attention has been sought after 48 hours from
symptom onset, patients be referred for evaluation at a
specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic or alternatively an outpatient
clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd within 24 hours of initial
presentation
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA without
high-risk featuresa undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of
antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as
possible and no later than 24 hours after initial presentation,
followed by referral to an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention
servicesd for comprehensive evaluation and management within 1
month of symptom onset
2.4.2 Some TIA patients, such as those who present with fluctuating or
crescendo hemibodye motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech
difficulty, should be evaluated in the ED to be considered for admission
to hospital
2.4.3 Patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke who are not on
an antiplatelet agent at the time of presentation should be started on
antiplatelet therapy immediately with 1 of the following after brain
imaging has excluded intracranial hemorrhage:
• ASA 160 mg loading dose, followed by enteric coated ASA (81–
325 mg) daily. Most patients should be on a maintenance dose of
81 mg/day
• clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose, followed by 75 mg/day
• extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg/ASA 25 mg bid (load with
ASA 160 mg first)
2.4.4 Rectal or gastric ASA should be offered as necessary, such as
for patients who fail swallowing screening
2.5–2.8 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; Australia, Australian Clinical analysis; ED, emergency department; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; OHTAC, Ontario Health Technology Advisory Committee; NHS/NICE,
National Collaborating Centre for Chronic Conditions; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack.
aSymptoms consistent with a hemispheric event, including sudden hemiparesis, speech difficulties, or monocular vision loss, and/or known high
risk conditions associated with stroke, including atrial fibrillation (especially if inadequately anticoagulated) or known carotid artery
atherosclerosis with > 50% stenosis (narrowing) on the side consistent with the hemispheric event.
bPatients with very mild persistent symptoms or no residual symptoms but with a small asymptomatic infarct (stroke) on imaging
cA clinic with stroke expertise and the resources to conduct all necessary investigations in 1 place to initiate rapid treatment, including brain and
vascular imaging, heart monitoring, and laboratory tests.
dFor example, a provincial stroke-prevention clinic or community neurology/internal medicine clinic with a stroke-prevention focus.
eMotor weakness may be in 1 body segment (face, arm, or leg), but sensory loss must involve at least 2 contiguous segments (face/arm or
arm/leg) on 1 side of the body to be considered highest risk.

The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel.
Module 2 Implementation Considerations
General considerations
• Standardized priority protocols should be established for all suspected stroke patients to receive treatment as soon as
possible and be admitted to a stroke unit within the first few hours after presenting to an ED
• Hospitals should have ready access to rectal ASA
Abbreviations: ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; ED, emergency department..

                    </branch>

          </exclusiveGateway>

                    <branch condition='Swallowing screening failed or oral route is unavailable' id='branch33'>
                      <activity role='ED physician and nurse' action='offer rectal or gastric ASA as necessary for the ASA loading dose and plan extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg and ASA 25 mg twice daily when oral route is available' objects='rectal or gastric ASA,planned dipyridamole and ASA therapy' id='activity35'/>
                    </branch>
                  </exclusiveGateway>
                </branch>
              </exclusiveGateway>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='Intracranial hemorrhage is not excluded, antiplatelet therapy is contraindicated, or patient is already on an antiplatelet agent at presentation' id='branch34'>
              <activity role='stroke team' action='continue individualized stroke-prevention management without new immediate antiplatelet initiation' objects='individualized management plan' id='activity36'/>
            </branch>
          </exclusiveGateway>
          <activity role='stroke team' action='tailor other stroke-prevention treatments to each patient' objects='individualized stroke-prevention treatment plan' id='activity22'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='High-risk TIA or minor stroke and medical attention sought more than 48 hours after symptom onset' id='branch38'>
          <activity role='ED physician' action='determine the most appropriate urgent referral destination' objects='referral destination options' id='activity23_1'/>
          <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway23_1'>
            <branch condition='Specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic access is available within 24 hours of initial presentation' id='branch23_1'>
              <activity role='ED physician' action='refer patient within 24 hours of initial presentation to a specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic for evaluation' objects='specialized clinic urgent referral' id='activity23_2'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='Specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic access is not available within 24 hours of initial presentation' id='branch23_2'>
              <activity role='ED physician' action='refer patient within 24 hours of initial presentation to an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention services for evaluation' objects='outpatient urgent referral' id='activity23_3'/>
            </branch>
          </exclusiveGateway>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='TIA without high-risk features' id='branch39'>
          <activity role='stroke team' action='classify patient as TIA without high-risk features' objects='risk stratification result' id='activity24'/>
          <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway4'>
            <branch id='branch24'>
              <activity role='ED physician and radiology team' action='arrange brain CT scan and antiplatelet initiation pathway as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after initial presentation' objects='brain CT request,antiplatelet initiation plan' id='activity25'/>
            </branch>
            <branch id='branch25'>
              <activity role='ED physician' action='refer patient to outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention services for comprehensive evaluation and management within 1 month of symptom onset' objects='outpatient referral' id='activity26'/>
            </branch>
          </parallelGateway>
          <activity role='ED physician and radiology team' action='review brain imaging to exclude intracranial hemorrhage when imaging is available' objects='brain imaging result' id='activity42'/>
          <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway10'>
            <branch condition='Intracranial hemorrhage is excluded, antiplatelet therapy is not contraindicated, and patient is not on an antiplatelet agent at presentation' id='branch40'>
              <activity role='ED physician' action='confirm need for immediate antiplatelet initiation' objects='antiplatelet eligibility review' id='activity43'/>
              <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway11'>
                <branch condition='Select ASA regimen' id='branch41'>
                  <activity role='ED physician' action='select ASA regimen' objects='ASA order set' id='activity44'/>
                  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway12'>
                    <branch condition='Swallowing screening passed' id='branch42'>
                      <activity role='ED physician and nurse' action='administer ASA 160 mg loading dose followed by enteric coated ASA 81-325 mg daily, with most patients on 81 mg per day maintenance' objects='ASA medication' id='activity45'/>
                    </branch>
                    <branch condition='Swallowing screening failed or oral route is unavailable' id='branch43'>
                      <activity role='ED physician and nurse' action='offer rectal or gastric ASA as necessary and administer ASA 160 mg loading dose with planned ASA maintenance therapy' objects='rectal or gastric ASA' id='activity46'/>
                    </branch>
                  </exclusiveGateway>
                </branch>
                <branch condition='Select clopidogrel regimen' id='branch44'>
                  <activity role='ED physician and nurse' action='administer clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose followed by 75 mg per day' objects='clopidogrel medication' id='activity47'/>
                </branch>
                <branch condition='Select extended-release dipyridamole and ASA regimen' id='branch45'>
                  <activity role='ED physician' action='select extended-release dipyridamole and ASA regimen' objects='dipyridamole and ASA order set' id='activity48'/>

                    </branch>

          </exclusiveGateway>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
      <activity role='stroke team' action='assess secondary prevention needs and risk factors for all patients whether admitted to hospital or discharged' objects='secondary prevention assessment' id='activity37_1'/>

| **task_prompt** | Module 2: Early Treatment This module identifies best practices for the ED treatment of TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke. The majority of TIA patients and some patients with minor stroke do not require admission to hospital and should be referred to an urgent TIA/minor stroke unit/TIA clinic/stroke-prevention clinic or comparable ambulatory care setting for rapid diagnostic and medical evaluation. All patients should be given appropriate cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies (Modules 5 and 10). Module 2 Recommended Practices  2.1 Glucose management 2.1.1 Hypoglycemia should be corrected immediately 2.1.2 Blood glucose measurement should be repeated if the first random glucose value is &gt; 11 mmol/L 2.2 Body temperature 2.2.1 Temperature should be evaluated as part of routine vital signs every 4 hours for first 48 hours For temperature &gt; 37.5 C: • initiate temperature-reducing measures • investigate potential infection • initiate antipyretic and antimicrobial therapy as required 2.3 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies 2.3.1 All patients, whether admitted to hospital or discharged, should be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary prevention assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10) 2.4 Management of patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke 2.4.1 The following OHTAC recommendations should be followed: • OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA with high- risk featuresa or a minor strokeb undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset, followed by other stroke-prevention treatments tailored to each patient. With respect to the location of care, OHTAC recommends that: o such immediate care be provided at a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinicc o where delays to accessing a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic pose risks to patient health, evaluation (as outlined above) occur at an appropriately resourced ED, and further consideration be given to inpatient evaluation and management for stroke prevention. OHTAC further recommends the establishment of accreditation standards for TIA/minor stroke care to ensure equitable access to appropriate, high-quality care, irrespective of the location of initial presentation o where medical attention has been sought after 48 hours from symptom onset, patients be referred for evaluation at a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic or alternatively an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd within 24 hours of initial presentation • OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA without high-risk featuresa undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after initial presentation, followed by referral to an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd for comprehensive evaluation and management within 1 month of symptom onset 2.4.2 Some TIA patients, such as those who present with fluctuating or crescendo hemibodye motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech difficulty, should be evaluated in the ED to be considered for admission to hospital 2.4.3 Patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke who are not on an antiplatelet agent at the time of presentation should be started on antiplatelet therapy immediately with 1 of the following after brain imaging has excluded intracranial hemorrhage: • ASA 160 mg loading dose, followed by enteric coated ASA (81– 325 mg) daily. Most patients should be on a maintenance dose of 81 mg/day • clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose, followed by 75 mg/day • extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg/ASA 25 mg bid (load with ASA 160 mg first) 2.4.4 Rectal or gastric ASA should be offered as necessary, such as for patients who fail swallowing screening 2.5–2.8 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; Australia, Australian Clinical analysis; ED, emergency department; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; OHTAC, Ontario Health Technology Advisory Committee; NHS/NICE, National Collaborating Centre for Chronic Conditions; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack. aSymptoms consistent with a hemispheric event, including sudden hemiparesis, speech difficulties, or monocular vision loss, and/or known high risk conditions associated with stroke, including atrial fibrillation (especially if inadequately anticoagulated) or known carotid artery atherosclerosis with &gt; 50% stenosis (narrowing) on the side consistent with the hemispheric event. bPatients with very mild persistent symptoms or no residual symptoms but with a small asymptomatic infarct (stroke) on imaging cA clinic with stroke expertise and the resources to conduct all necessary investigations in 1 place to initiate rapid treatment, including brain and vascular imaging, heart monitoring, and laboratory tests. dFor example, a provincial stroke-prevention clinic or community neurology/internal medicine clinic with a stroke-prevention focus. eMotor weakness may be in 1 body segment (face, arm, or leg), but sensory loss must involve at least 2 contiguous segments (face/arm or arm/leg) on 1 side of the body to be considered highest risk. The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel. Module 2 Implementation Considerations General considerations • Standardized priority protocols should be established for all suspected stroke patients to receive treatment as soon as possible and be admitted to a stroke unit within the first few hours after presenting to an ED • Hospitals should have ready access to rectal ASA Abbreviations: ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; ED, emergency department. |

The majority of TIA patients and some patients with minor stroke do not require admission to hospital and should be referred to an urgent TIA/minor stroke unit/TIA clinic/stroke-prevention clinic or comparable ambulatory care setting for rapid diagnostic and medical evaluation. All patients should be given appropriate cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies (Modules 5 and 10). With respect to the location of care, OHTAC recommends that: o such immediate care be provided at a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinicc o where delays to accessing a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic pose risks to patient health, evaluation (as outlined above) occur at an appropriately resourced ED, and further consideration be given to inpatient evaluation and management for stroke prevention. OHTAC further recommends the establishment of accreditation standards for TIA/minor stroke care to ensure equitable access to appropriate, high-quality care, irrespective of the location of initial presentation o where medical attention has been sought after 48 hours from symptom onset, patients be referred for evaluation at a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic or alternatively an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd within 24 hours of initial presentation • OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA without high-risk featuresa undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after initial presentation, followed by referral to an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd for comprehensive evaluation and management within 1 month of symptom onset 2.4.2 Some TIA patients, such as those who present with fluctuating or crescendo hemibodye motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech difficulty, should be evaluated in the ED to be considered for admission to hospital 2.4.3 Patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke who are not on an antiplatelet agent at the time of presentation should be started on antiplatelet therapy immediately with 1 of the following after brain imaging has excluded intracranial hemorrhage: • ASA 160 mg loading dose, followed by enteric coated ASA (81– 325 mg) daily. Most patients should be on a maintenance dose of 81 mg/day • clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose, followed by 75 mg/day • extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg/ASA 25 mg bid (load with ASA 160 mg first) 2.4.4 Rectal or gastric ASA should be offered as necessary, such as for patients who fail swallowing screening 2.5–2.8 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; Australia, Australian Clinical analysis; ED, emergency department; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; OHTAC, Ontario Health Technology Advisory Committee; NHS/NICE, National Collaborating Centre for Chronic Conditions; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack. aSymptoms consistent with a hemispheric event, including sudden hemiparesis, speech difficulties, or monocular vision loss, and/or known high risk conditions associated with stroke, including atrial fibrillation (especially if inadequately anticoagulated) or known carotid artery atherosclerosis with &gt; 50% stenosis (narrowing) on the side consistent with the hemispheric event. bPatients with very mild persistent symptoms or no residual symptoms but with a small asymptomatic infarct (stroke) on imaging cA clinic with stroke expertise and the resources to conduct all necessary investigations in 1 place to initiate rapid treatment, including brain and vascular imaging, heart monitoring, and laboratory tests. dFor example, a provincial stroke-prevention clinic or community neurology/internal medicine clinic with a stroke-prevention focus. eMotor weakness may be in 1 body segment (face, arm, or leg), but sensory loss must involve at least 2 contiguous segments (face/arm or arm/leg) on 1 side of the body to be considered highest risk. The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel. Module 2 Implementation Considerations General considerations • Standardized priority protocols should be established for all suspected stroke patients to receive treatment as soon as possible and be admitted to a stroke unit within the first few hours after presenting to an ED • Hospitals should have ready access to rectal ASA Abbreviations: ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; ED, emergency department. |

[2026-07-04 22:48:01 INFO] Team leader: **[Start Chat]**

[You're a process design reviewer. Now, we share a common interest in collaborating to successfully complete a task assigned by a new customer.
You are familiar with exclusive gateway, parallel gateway and inclusive gateway in BPMN. Your main responsibilities include give suggestion to the process.
Here is a new customer's task: Module 2: Early Treatment
This module identifies best practices for the ED treatment of TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke. The
majority of TIA patients and some patients with minor stroke do not require admission to hospital
and should be referred to an urgent TIA/minor stroke unit/TIA clinic/stroke-prevention clinic or
comparable ambulatory care setting for rapid diagnostic and medical evaluation. All patients should
be given appropriate cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies (Modules 5 and 10).
Module 2 Recommended Practices 
2.1 Glucose management
2.1.1 Hypoglycemia should be corrected immediately
2.1.2 Blood glucose measurement should be repeated if the first
random glucose value is > 11 mmol/L
2.2 Body temperature
2.2.1 Temperature should be evaluated as part of routine vital signs
every 4 hours for first 48 hours
For temperature > 37.5 C:
• initiate temperature-reducing measures
• investigate potential infection
• initiate antipyretic and antimicrobial therapy as required
2.3 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies
2.3.1 All patients, whether admitted to hospital or discharged, should
be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary prevention
assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10)
2.4 Management of patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
2.4.1 The following OHTAC recommendations should be followed:
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA with high-
risk featuresa or a minor strokeb undergo a brain CT scan and
initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated)
as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset,
followed by other stroke-prevention treatments tailored to each
patient. With respect to the location of care, OHTAC recommends
that:
o such immediate care be provided at a specialized TIA/minor
stroke clinicc
o where delays to accessing a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic
pose risks to patient health, evaluation (as outlined above) occur
at an appropriately resourced ED, and further consideration be
given to inpatient evaluation and management for stroke
prevention. OHTAC further recommends the establishment of
accreditation standards for TIA/minor stroke care to ensure
equitable access to appropriate, high-quality care, irrespective of
the location of initial presentation
o where medical attention has been sought after 48 hours from
symptom onset, patients be referred for evaluation at a
specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic or alternatively an outpatient
clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd within 24 hours of initial
presentation
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA without
high-risk featuresa undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of
antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as
possible and no later than 24 hours after initial presentation,
followed by referral to an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention
servicesd for comprehensive evaluation and management within 1
month of symptom onset
2.4.2 Some TIA patients, such as those who present with fluctuating or
crescendo hemibodye motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech
difficulty, should be evaluated in the ED to be considered for admission
to hospital
2.4.3 Patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke who are not on
an antiplatelet agent at the time of presentation should be started on
antiplatelet therapy immediately with 1 of the following after brain
imaging has excluded intracranial hemorrhage:
• ASA 160 mg loading dose, followed by enteric coated ASA (81–
325 mg) daily. Most patients should be on a maintenance dose of
81 mg/day
• clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose, followed by 75 mg/day
• extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg/ASA 25 mg bid (load with
ASA 160 mg first)
2.4.4 Rectal or gastric ASA should be offered as necessary, such as
for patients who fail swallowing screening
2.5–2.8 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; Australia, Australian Clinical analysis; ED, emergency department; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; OHTAC, Ontario Health Technology Advisory Committee; NHS/NICE,
National Collaborating Centre for Chronic Conditions; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack.
aSymptoms consistent with a hemispheric event, including sudden hemiparesis, speech difficulties, or monocular vision loss, and/or known high
risk conditions associated with stroke, including atrial fibrillation (especially if inadequately anticoagulated) or known carotid artery
atherosclerosis with > 50% stenosis (narrowing) on the side consistent with the hemispheric event.
bPatients with very mild persistent symptoms or no residual symptoms but with a small asymptomatic infarct (stroke) on imaging
cA clinic with stroke expertise and the resources to conduct all necessary investigations in 1 place to initiate rapid treatment, including brain and
vascular imaging, heart monitoring, and laboratory tests.
dFor example, a provincial stroke-prevention clinic or community neurology/internal medicine clinic with a stroke-prevention focus.
eMotor weakness may be in 1 body segment (face, arm, or leg), but sensory loss must involve at least 2 contiguous segments (face/arm or
arm/leg) on 1 side of the body to be considered highest risk.

The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel.
Module 2 Implementation Considerations
General considerations
• Standardized priority protocols should be established for all suspected stroke patients to receive treatment as soon as
possible and be admitted to a stroke unit within the first few hours after presenting to an ED
• Hospitals should have ready access to rectal ASA
Abbreviations: ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; ED, emergency department..
To complete the task, I will give you one or more instructions, and you must help me to write a specific solution that appropriately solves the requested instruction based on your expertise and my needs.]

task: Module 2: Early Treatment
This module identifies best practices for the ED treatment of TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke. The
majority of TIA patients and some patients with minor stroke do not require admission to hospital
and should be referred to an urgent TIA/minor stroke unit/TIA clinic/stroke-prevention clinic or
comparable ambulatory care setting for rapid diagnostic and medical evaluation. All patients should
be given appropriate cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies (Modules 5 and 10).
Module 2 Recommended Practices 
2.1 Glucose management
2.1.1 Hypoglycemia should be corrected immediately
2.1.2 Blood glucose measurement should be repeated if the first
random glucose value is > 11 mmol/L
2.2 Body temperature
2.2.1 Temperature should be evaluated as part of routine vital signs
every 4 hours for first 48 hours
For temperature > 37.5 C:
• initiate temperature-reducing measures
• investigate potential infection
• initiate antipyretic and antimicrobial therapy as required
2.3 Cross-continuum prevention assessment and therapies
2.3.1 All patients, whether admitted to hospital or discharged, should
be given appropriate cross-continuum secondary prevention
assessments and therapies (Modules 5 and 10)
2.4 Management of patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
2.4.1 The following OHTAC recommendations should be followed:
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA with high-
risk featuresa or a minor strokeb undergo a brain CT scan and
initiation of antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated)
as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset,
followed by other stroke-prevention treatments tailored to each
patient. With respect to the location of care, OHTAC recommends
that:
o such immediate care be provided at a specialized TIA/minor
stroke clinicc
o where delays to accessing a specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic
pose risks to patient health, evaluation (as outlined above) occur
at an appropriately resourced ED, and further consideration be
given to inpatient evaluation and management for stroke
prevention. OHTAC further recommends the establishment of
accreditation standards for TIA/minor stroke care to ensure
equitable access to appropriate, high-quality care, irrespective of
the location of initial presentation
o where medical attention has been sought after 48 hours from
symptom onset, patients be referred for evaluation at a
specialized TIA/minor stroke clinic or alternatively an outpatient
clinic with stroke-prevention servicesd within 24 hours of initial
presentation
• OHTAC recommends that patients presenting with a TIA without
high-risk featuresa undergo a brain CT scan and initiation of
antiplatelet therapy (provided this is not contraindicated) as soon as
possible and no later than 24 hours after initial presentation,
followed by referral to an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention
servicesd for comprehensive evaluation and management within 1
month of symptom onset
2.4.2 Some TIA patients, such as those who present with fluctuating or
crescendo hemibodye motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech
difficulty, should be evaluated in the ED to be considered for admission
to hospital
2.4.3 Patients with TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke who are not on
an antiplatelet agent at the time of presentation should be started on
antiplatelet therapy immediately with 1 of the following after brain
imaging has excluded intracranial hemorrhage:
• ASA 160 mg loading dose, followed by enteric coated ASA (81–
325 mg) daily. Most patients should be on a maintenance dose of
81 mg/day
• clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose, followed by 75 mg/day
• extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg/ASA 25 mg bid (load with
ASA 160 mg first)
2.4.4 Rectal or gastric ASA should be offered as necessary, such as
for patients who fail swallowing screening
2.5–2.8 Recommendations are not applicable to TIA or minor (nondisabling) stroke
Abbreviations: AHA/ASA, American Heart Association/American Stroke Association; ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; Australia, Australian Clinical analysis; ED, emergency department; HQO, Health Quality Ontario; OHTAC, Ontario Health Technology Advisory Committee; NHS/NICE,
National Collaborating Centre for Chronic Conditions; SIGN, Scottish Intercollegiate Guidelines Network; TIA, transient ischemic attack.
aSymptoms consistent with a hemispheric event, including sudden hemiparesis, speech difficulties, or monocular vision loss, and/or known high
risk conditions associated with stroke, including atrial fibrillation (especially if inadequately anticoagulated) or known carotid artery
atherosclerosis with > 50% stenosis (narrowing) on the side consistent with the hemispheric event.
bPatients with very mild persistent symptoms or no residual symptoms but with a small asymptomatic infarct (stroke) on imaging
cA clinic with stroke expertise and the resources to conduct all necessary investigations in 1 place to initiate rapid treatment, including brain and
vascular imaging, heart monitoring, and laboratory tests.
dFor example, a provincial stroke-prevention clinic or community neurology/internal medicine clinic with a stroke-prevention focus.
eMotor weakness may be in 1 body segment (face, arm, or leg), but sensory loss must involve at least 2 contiguous segments (face/arm or
arm/leg) on 1 side of the body to be considered highest risk.

The following implementation considerations were noted by members of the expert advisory panel.
Module 2 Implementation Considerations
General considerations
• Standardized priority protocols should be established for all suspected stroke patients to receive treatment as soon as
possible and be admitted to a stroke unit within the first few hours after presenting to an ED
• Hospitals should have ready access to rectal ASA
Abbreviations: ASA, acetylsalicylic acid; ED, emergency department.
process text:
<process>
  <activity role='ED team' action='activate standardized priority protocol and record symptom onset and presentation time' objects='suspected TIA or minor stroke patient record' id='activity1'/>
  <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway1'>
    <branch id='branch1'>
      <activity role='ED nurse' action='measure random blood glucose' objects='blood glucose sample' id='activity2'/>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway1'>
        <branch condition='Blood glucose indicates hypoglycemia' id='branch6'>
          <activity role='ED nurse' action='correct hypoglycemia immediately' objects='hypoglycemia treatment' id='activity3'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='First random glucose value > 11 mmol/L' id='branch7'>
          <activity role='ED nurse' action='repeat blood glucose measurement' objects='repeat blood glucose sample' id='activity4'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='Otherwise' id='branch8'>
          <activity role='ED nurse' action='continue glucose management' objects='glucose monitoring record' id='activity5'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch2'>
      <activity role='ED nurse' action='evaluate temperature as part of routine vital signs every 4 hours for the first 48 hours' objects='vital signs record' id='activity6'/>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway2'>
        <branch condition='Temperature > 37.5 C' id='branch9'>
          <activity role='ED nurse' action='identify elevated temperature' objects='temperature result' id='activity7'/>
          <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway2'>
            <branch id='branch10'>
              <activity role='clinical staff' action='initiate temperature-reducing measures' objects='cooling measures' id='activity8'/>
            </branch>
            <branch id='branch11'>
              <activity role='clinical staff' action='investigate potential infection' objects='infection assessment' id='activity9'/>
            </branch>
            <branch id='branch12'>
              <activity role='physician' action='assess requirement for antipyretic and antimicrobial therapy' objects='fever treatment plan' id='activity10'/>
              <inclusiveGateway id='inclusivegateway1'>
                <branch condition='Antipyretic therapy is required' id='branch13'>
                  <activity role='physician' action='initiate antipyretic therapy' objects='antipyretic medication' id='activity11'/>
                </branch>
                <branch condition='Antimicrobial therapy is required' id='branch14'>
                  <activity role='physician' action='initiate antimicrobial therapy' objects='antimicrobial medication' id='activity12'/>
                </branch>
              </inclusiveGateway>
            </branch>
          </parallelGateway>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='Temperature is 37.5 C or lower' id='branch15'>
          <activity role='ED nurse' action='continue routine temperature monitoring every 4 hours for the first 48 hours' objects='temperature monitoring record' id='activity13'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
    </branch>
    <branch id='branch3'>
      <activity role='ED physician and stroke team' action='assess neurologic presentation for high-risk TIA features, minor stroke status, and fluctuating or crescendo symptoms' objects='clinical assessment and neurologic examination' id='activity14_1'/>
      <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway14_1'>
        <branch id='branch14_1'>
          <activity role='ED physician' action='review current antiplatelet use and antiplatelet contraindications' objects='medication history and contraindication review' id='activity14_2'/>
        </branch>
        <branch id='branch14_2'>
          <activity role='ED nurse' action='perform swallowing screening' objects='swallowing screen result' id='activity14_3'/>
        </branch>
        <branch id='branch14_3'>
          <activity role='ED team' action='verify symptom onset time and time since onset' objects='symptom timing record' id='activity14_4'/>
        </branch>
      </parallelGateway>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway8'>
        <branch condition='TIA presents with fluctuating or crescendo hemibody motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech difficulty' id='branch35'>
          <activity role='ED physician' action='perform urgent reassessment of fluctuating or crescendo deficits and clinical stability' objects='urgent reassessment findings' id='activity39'/>
          <activity role='ED physician and stroke team' action='consult the stroke team and document whether hospital admission should be considered' objects='admission consideration note' id='activity40'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='No fluctuating or crescendo hemibody motor weakness, sensory loss, or speech difficulty' id='branch36'>
          <activity role='ED physician and stroke team' action='document absence of fluctuating or crescendo symptoms and continue disposition pathway determination' objects='symptom pattern assessment record' id='activity41'/>
        </branch>
      </exclusiveGateway>
      <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway9'>
        <branch condition='High-risk TIA or minor stroke and medical attention sought within 48 hours from symptom onset' id='branch37'>
          <activity role='stroke team' action='classify patient as high-risk TIA or minor stroke presenting within 48 hours' objects='risk stratification result' id='activity16'/>
          <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway3'>
            <branch id='branch18'>
              <activity role='ED physician and radiology team' action='arrange brain CT scan and antiplatelet initiation pathway as soon as possible and no later than 24 hours after symptom onset' objects='brain CT request,antiplatelet initiation plan' id='activity17'/>
            </branch>
            <branch id='branch19'>
              <activity role='stroke coordinator' action='determine location of immediate care' objects='care location options' id='activity18'/>
              <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway3'>
                <branch condition='Timely access to a specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic is available' id='branch20'>
                  <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway19_1'>
                    <branch id='branch19_1'>
                      <activity role='specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic team' action='complete brain and vascular imaging' objects='brain imaging and vascular imaging results' id='activity19_1'/>
                    </branch>
                    <branch id='branch19_2'>
                      <activity role='specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic team' action='perform heart monitoring and laboratory tests' objects='heart monitoring record and laboratory results' id='activity19_2'/>
                    </branch>
                  </parallelGateway>
                  <activity role='specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic team' action='consolidate specialized clinic findings for immediate care' objects='specialized clinic evaluation summary' id='activity19_3'/>
                </branch>
                <branch condition='Delay to specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic poses risk to patient health' id='branch21'>
                  <parallelGateway id='parallelgateway20_1'>
                    <branch id='branch20_1'>
                      <activity role='ED team' action='perform urgent neurologic and stroke-prevention evaluation in the ED' objects='ED stroke assessment' id='activity20_1'/>
                    </branch>

                      <activity role='ED team' action='complete available vascular imaging, heart monitoring, and laboratory tests' objects='vascular imaging results,heart monitoring record,laboratory results' id='activity20_2'/>
                    </branch>
                  </parallelGateway>
                  <activity role='ED physician and stroke team' action='integrate ED evaluation findings for stroke-prevention management' objects='integrated ED evaluation summary' id='activity20_3'/>
                  <activity role='ED physician and stroke team' action='consider inpatient evaluation and management for stroke prevention' objects='inpatient evaluation plan' id='activity21'/>
                </branch>
              </exclusiveGateway>
            </branch>
          </parallelGateway>
          <activity role='ED physician and radiology team' action='review brain imaging to exclude intracranial hemorrhage when imaging is available' objects='brain imaging result' id='activity27'/>
          <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway4'>
            <branch condition='Intracranial hemorrhage is excluded, antiplatelet therapy is not contraindicated, and patient is not on an antiplatelet agent at presentation' id='branch26'>
              <activity role='ED physician' action='confirm need for immediate antiplatelet initiation' objects='antiplatelet eligibility review' id='activity28'/>
              <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway5'>
                <branch condition='Select ASA regimen' id='branch27'>
                  <activity role='ED physician' action='select ASA regimen' objects='ASA order set' id='activity29'/>
                  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway6'>
                    <branch condition='Swallowing screening passed' id='branch28'>
                      <activity role='ED physician and nurse' action='administer ASA 160 mg loading dose followed by enteric coated ASA 81-325 mg daily, with most patients on 81 mg per day maintenance' objects='ASA medication' id='activity30'/>
                    </branch>
                    <branch condition='Swallowing screening failed or oral route is unavailable' id='branch29'>
                      <activity role='ED physician and nurse' action='offer rectal or gastric ASA as necessary and administer ASA 160 mg loading dose with planned ASA maintenance therapy' objects='rectal or gastric ASA' id='activity31'/>
                    </branch>
                  </exclusiveGateway>
                </branch>
                <branch condition='Select clopidogrel regimen' id='branch30'>
                  <activity role='ED physician and nurse' action='administer clopidogrel 300 mg loading dose followed by 75 mg per day' objects='clopidogrel medication' id='activity32'/>
                </branch>
                <branch condition='Select extended-release dipyridamole and ASA regimen' id='branch31'>
                  <activity role='ED physician' action='select extended-release dipyridamole and ASA regimen' objects='dipyridamole and ASA order set' id='activity33'/>
                  <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway7'>
                    <branch condition='Swallowing screening passed' id='branch32'>
                      <activity role='ED physician and nurse' action='administer ASA 160 mg loading dose then extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg and ASA 25 mg twice daily' objects='ASA medication,extended-release dipyridamole and ASA medication' id='activity34'/>
                    </branch>
                    <branch condition='Swallowing screening failed or oral route is unavailable' id='branch33'>
                      <activity role='ED physician and nurse' action='offer rectal or gastric ASA as necessary for the ASA loading dose and plan extended-release dipyridamole 200 mg and ASA 25 mg twice daily when oral route is available' objects='rectal or gastric ASA,planned dipyridamole and ASA therapy' id='activity35'/>
                    </branch>
                  </exclusiveGateway>
                </branch>
              </exclusiveGateway>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='Intracranial hemorrhage is not excluded, antiplatelet therapy is contraindicated, or patient is already on an antiplatelet agent at presentation' id='branch34'>
              <activity role='stroke team' action='continue individualized stroke-prevention management without new immediate antiplatelet initiation' objects='individualized management plan' id='activity36'/>
            </branch>
          </exclusiveGateway>
          <activity role='stroke team' action='tailor other stroke-prevention treatments to each patient' objects='individualized stroke-prevention treatment plan' id='activity22'/>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='High-risk TIA or minor stroke and medical attention sought more than 48 hours after symptom onset' id='branch38'>
          <activity role='ED physician' action='determine the most appropriate urgent referral destination' objects='referral destination options' id='activity23_1'/>
          <exclusiveGateway id='exclusivegateway23_1'>

              <activity role='ED physician' action='refer patient within 24 hours of initial presentation to a specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic for evaluation' objects='specialized clinic urgent referral' id='activity23_2'/>
            </branch>
            <branch condition='Specialized TIA or minor stroke clinic access is not available within 24 hours of initial presentation' id='branch23_2'>
              <activity role='ED physician' action='refer patient within 24 hours of initial presentation to an outpatient clinic with stroke-prevention services for evaluation' objects='outpatient urgent referral' id='activity23_3'/>
            </branch>
          </exclusiveGateway>
        </branch>
        <branch condition='TIA without high-risk features' id='branch39'>
